# Draft Paper Section — Mathematical and Empirical Proof

Manufactured traction cannot be proven as a universal empirical law because platforms do not disclose their complete ranking functions and because user response varies across contexts. However, the core mechanism can be proven conditionally. Digital markets allocate attention, trust, money, and legitimacy through proxy signals. If an allocation rule is monotone in a manipulable proxy, then artificial movement in that proxy can alter allocation whenever the movement crosses a decision boundary.

This simple proof is strengthened by feedback dynamics. When exposure creates engagement and engagement creates future exposure, an initial artificial boost can create cumulative downstream exposure even if the boost eventually decays. Recommender-system research describes this as a feedback-loop problem: systems are trained or evaluated on behaviour already shaped by algorithmic exposure, producing algorithmic confounding and popularity bias.

The human side has even stronger causal evidence. Randomized experiments show that visible popularity can bias later judgment. Muchnik, Aral, and Taylor found that an artificially added positive vote increased later positive voting by 32% and increased final scores by 25% on average. Salganik, Dodds, and Watts found that visible popularity information in an artificial music market increased inequality and unpredictability of success. These studies show that social proof is not merely decorative; it changes collective outcomes.

The market side is also measurable. NBER experimental evidence shows that fake reviews can shift consumers toward lower-quality products and create welfare losses. Structural modelling of Amazon reviews finds that fake reviews reduce consumer welfare and shift sales from honest to dishonest sellers. Streaming-fraud cases show the same logic in monetary form: if payouts are proportional to measured streams, fake streams can divert money from a royalty pool.

The strongest conclusion is therefore not that every manipulation succeeds. The defensible conclusion is that manufactured traction is a predictable failure mode of systems that allocate value through visible proxy signals. The same mathematical pattern recurs across social feeds, reviews, launch platforms, app stores, search, ads, music streaming, and startup metrics: distort the proxy, trigger the system, and convert appearance into value.
